revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Amano-Iwato]][[es:Ama-no-Iwato]][[fa:آما-نو-ایواتو]][[fr:Amano-Iwato]][[it:Ama-no-Iwato]][[ja:天岩戸]][[ko:아마노이와토]][[mni:ꯑꯃꯅꯣ-ꯏꯋꯇꯣ]][[pl:Amanoiwato]][[ru:Ама-но Ивато]][[th:อามะ โนะ อิวาโตะ]][[uk:Амано-Івато]][[zh:天岩戶]]


{{short description|Cave in Japanese mythology|qq=}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019|qq=}}
{{More citations needed|date=November 2015|qq=}}
[[File:Origin_of_Iwato_Kagura_Dance_Amaterasu_by_Toyokuni_III_(Kunisada)_1856.png|thumb|390x390px|Amaterasu emerges from the Heavenly Rock Cave, bringing sunlight back to the world (Triptych by {{ill|Kunisada|en|Kunisada|qid=Q467427|lt=Kunisada|1=Kunisada}})]]
{{nihongo|'''Ama-no-Iwato'''|天の岩戸||literally "heaven's rock cave"|qq=}} is a {{ill|Cave|en|Cave|qid=Q35509|lt=Cave|1=Cave}} in [[Japanese mythology]]. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'' (''Records of Ancient Matters'') and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the bad behavior of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susano'o]], the Japanese god of storms, drove his sister [[Amaterasu]] into the Ama-no-Iwato cave.  The land was thus deprived of light.<ref>Philippi, Donald L. 1968/1969. ''Kojiki''. Princeton, N.J.: Princeton University Press and Tokyo: University of Tokyo Press.</ref>
In order to get Amaterasu out of the cave the other gods called [[Kami|Yao-yorozu-no-kami]] threw a party outside with wisdom of [[Omoikane|Omoikane]]. The goddess [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame-no-Uzume-no-Mikoto]] performed a lewd dance, eliciting laughter. Amaterasu grew curious about the source of amusement and peeked out of the cave entrance. She became fascinated by her own reflection in the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|qid=Q327465|lt=Yata no Kagami|1=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} mirror which the other gods had crafted and hung before the cave for that purpose, and stood transfixed. {{ill|Ame-no-Tajikarao|en|Ame-no-Tajikarao|qid=Q849412|lt=Ame-no-Tajikarao|1=Ame-no-Tajikarao}} then forced the cave open and the world was bathed in light once again. As Amaterasu stepped out of the cave a holy seal was applied to it so that she could never go back into hiding.
==Higashihongu==
The main shrine called Higashihongu (east hall) and a hall of worship called Nishihongu (west hall) face each other across the Iwato River gorge. The Amano Iwato cave is an object of worship in festivals and is a rock cave on the other side of the Iwato River from Nishihongu. You can see the cave from Nishihongu after participating in a Shinto ritual for purification. Photography of any kind is prohibited.<ref name="JNTO">{{cite web|title=Amano-iwato Shrine - Japan National Tourism Organization|url=https://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/spot/shritemp/amano-iwato-shrine.html|website=Japan national tourism organization|access-date=3 May 2017|language=en|qq=}}</ref>
The grounds contain many plants and old trees. There are also rare ancient {{ill|Ginkgo|en|Ginkgo|qid=Q149461|lt=Ginkgo|1=Ginkgo}} and ''{{ill|Magnolia compressa|en|Magnolia compressa|qid=Q11291825|lt=Magnolia compressa|1=Magnolia compressa}}'' trees, which have been considered sacred in Japan since ancient times.
[[Image:Amanoyasugawara-panorama.jpg|thumb|600px|center|The [[Takamagahara]] pantheon is thought to have gathered and discussed how to get Amaterasu out of the cave at this riverside, {{nihongo|天安河原|Ama no Yasugawara|qq=}}, at [[Amanoiwato Shrine|Amanoiwato-jinja]] in {{ill|Takachiho, Miyazaki|en|Takachiho, Miyazaki|qid=Q1350939|lt=Takachiho|1=Takachiho, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}.]]
==See also==
